title: Prunus plena
intro: Prunus plena, also known as the double-flowered plum, is a deciduous shrub or small tree that belongs to the Rosaceae family. The health benefits of Prunus plena include its potential to act as an antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial agent, which may help alleviate various health issues such as digestive problems and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Prunus plena include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Prunus plena is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are commonly used to treat colds, coughs, and other minor health issues.
taxonomy: Prunus plena is classified within the family Rosaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the rose family. The genus Prunus is a group of flowering plants that includes various species of plums, cherries, and apricots. Prunus plena is further classified within the subgenus Prunus, which includes several species of plums and cherries. Within the subgenus Prunus, Prunus plena is often placed in the section Prunus, which is a group of closely related species. The classification of Prunus plena is still a subject of ongoing research and may be subject to revision as new information becomes available.

Prunus plena may help to reduce fever, alleviate digestive issues, and even support immune system function. Its antioxidants may also contribute to protecting against cell damage and promoting overall well-being. However, more research is needed to fully understand the extent of its health benefits and potential interactions with other medications.

side_effects: Prunus plena, a type of flowering cherry tree, is generally considered non-toxic and safe for consumption. However, eating the flowers, leaves, or unripe fruit of the Prunus plena tree can cause gastrointestinal upset in some individuals. The seeds, pits, or kernels of the tree's fruit are considered more toxic due to their amygdalin content, which can release cyanide when ingested. Ingestion of large quantities of these seeds can lead to cyanide poisoning, a potentially life-threatening condition. It is essential to exercise caution when handling or consuming any part of the Prunus plena tree.
